Neither variable is used afterwards. Likely leftover from a prior version of the subsetting logic. Question: Should lines 73–75 be deleted, or is this a WhichCells filter you plan to complete?

The name implies a subset but the full object is used throughout the file. Question: Should the subset be re-enabled, or should astro_sub be renamed to astro and all downstream references updated?

title: CLAUDE.md
text:
This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.


Differential brain effects of anti-Aβ antibody treatment on Amyloid-Related Imaging Abnormalities (ARIA) in a mouse model expressing human APOE4.

Platform: 10x Genomics Xenium In Situ spatial transcriptomics (480-gene targeted panel)
Samples: 6 mouse brains (C57BL/6J with human APOE4 targeted replacement) across 2 slides
Conditions: Aducanumab (anti-Aβ antibody) vs IgG control Slide 1 (0021991): KK4_465 (IgG), KK4_492 (Adu), KK4_504 (IgG) Slide 2 (0021998): KK4_496 (IgG), KK4_502 (Adu), KK4_464 (Adu)




Individual reports: quarto render <file>.qmd




20260217-helper_functions.R — Reusable R functions (fiddle, anayet, scProp)
seurat_objects/ — Post-RCTD/SPLIT Seurat objects (qs2 format, not tracked in git)
results/ — Saved RDS outputs from DE analyses
docs/ — Supporting images (e.g., brain region reference)



Scripts: YYYYMMDD-description.{R|qmd}



Original objects (20260213) had metadata errors and were replaced with corrected 20260217 versions.







File
Contents




20260217_Part2_fullobj.qs2
Full object, all cell types


20260217_Part2_immune.qs2
Immune cell subset


20260225-mg_kai.qs2
Microglia subset (Kai’s annotation, canonical)


20260225-mg_kai_slide{1,2}.qs2
Per-slide microglia


20260217_Part2_fullobj_slide{1,2}.qs2
Per-slide full objects


20260305-astro_0.3.qs2
Reclustered astrocytes (resolution 0.3, SCT + PCA + UMAP)













Column
Values / Notes




sample_ID
KK4_465, KK4_492, KK4_504, KK4_496, KK4_502, KK4_464


Treatment.Group
“Adu” or “IgG”


Region
Brain region (may contain NA)


annotatedclusters
Major cell types (Endothelial, Astrocyte, Oligodendrocyte, OPC, etc.)


annotated_subclusters
Immune subtypes with spaces (e.g., “Activated DAM”, “Border-like Microglia”)


sub.cluster
Microglia subclusters (in Kai’s mg object)







Xenium — Raw counts only (no data layer). Use for pseudobulk DESeq2.
SCT — SCT-normalized. Use for visualization/normalization.
Xenium_SPLIT, SCT_SPLIT — SPLIT-decontaminated versions.





Three main functions, each with a _help() companion:








Function
Purpose
Key packages




fiddle()
Multi-faceted violin plots with colored cluster labels on y-axis
ggplot2, grid, scales


anayet()
Differential expression (FindMarkers + DESeq2) with auto-scaled volcano plots
Seurat, EnhancedVolcano


scProp()
Cluster proportion summaries per sample, with optional compareGroups table
ggplot2, compareGroups



Also includes density_decor() for consistent density plot theming.
Default metadata columns expected: final_clusters_clean (cluster), Mouse.ID (sample), Treatment.Group (treatment). Override via function arguments when using objects with different column names.



The DEG qmd files (MG, endothelial, astrocytes, oligo, OPC) share an identical structure:

Load Seurat object → subset to cell type → set Xenium as default assay
Map over brain regions: check replication (≥2 samples/group), aggregate pseudobulk via AggregateExpression(group.by = "sample_ID", return.seurat = TRUE)
Call anayet() with DESeq2 for each region
Summary visualizations: DEG count bar plot, cross-region log2FC heatmap, effect size dot plot
Save results list to results/ as RDS





AggregateExpression() converts underscores to hyphens in column names (KK4_465 → KK4-465). Handle with gsub("_", "-", ...) when mapping treatment metadata.
FindMarkers(test.use = "DESeq2") on SCT assay triggers CheckDots error — use Xenium assay.
Xenium assay only has counts layer (no data). Pseudobulk via AggregateExpression(return.seurat = TRUE) creates both layers automatically.
480-gene panel limits GSEA: use minSize = 5 (not default 15) in fgsea().





Seurat (v5), tidyverse, grid, scales, forcats
EnhancedVolcano, compareGroups, speckle, kableExtra
DESeq2, fgsea, msigdbr — for DE and pathway analysis
No renv lockfile — packages managed via library() calls





Use the /coding-standard skill
in-line annotate the code (on top)
do not include package operator :: (dplyr::, purr::). Load the packages if necessary.






All commits must follow this structure:
Title line summarizing the change

Bullet point explaining specific change
Bullet point explaining another change
Additional bullets as needed

The title should be a brief, complete sentence. Leave one blank line between the title and the bullet points. Each bullet point describes a specific change made in the commit. IMPORTANT: do not include messages attributing the commit to Claude (e.g., “Committed by Claude”, “Claude made changes”, “Generated with Claude Code”, etc.).



Changes to analysis code, new visualizations, parameter adjustments, and data processing steps require bullet points explaining what was done. Changes to documentation, comments, or markdown text can use simple title-only messages without bullets.



Add differential expression analysis for treatment vs control

- Ran DESeq2 with default parameters on normalized counts
- Filtered results for padj < 0.05 and log2FC > 1
- Generated volcano plot saved to figures/volcano_treatment.png
- Exported significant genes to results/DE_genes_treatment.csv



Update README with installation instructions

title: Cell types composition in the full object
text:
Code
library(qs2)
library(Seurat)
library(tidyverse)
source("20260217-helper_functions.R")


full <- qs_read("seurat_objects/20260217_Part2_fullobj.qs2")
md <- full@meta.data



Cell type composition across brain regions and treatment groups
To characterise the cellular landscape captured by the Xenium 480-gene spatial transcriptomics panel, the total number of cells assigned to each major cell type (annotatedclusters) was quantified per brain region and stratified by treatment group (aducanumab, Adu; isotype control, IgG). Cell type annotations were derived from the seurat full object curated by Chloe


Code
n_cells <- md |>
  group_by(annotatedclusters,Region, Treatment.Group) |>
  summarise(n_cells = n())

ggplot(n_cells, aes(x = Region , y = n_cells, fill = Treatment.Group))+
  geom_col()+
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(scale_cut = cut_short_scale()))+
  facet_wrap(~annotatedclusters, scales = "free_y", ncol = 4)+
  theme_bw()+
  theme(legend.position = "top",
  axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 40, hjust = 1))

Pathway analysis

Microglia GSEA — Gene set enrichment by brain region (MSigDB M7 Immunologic Signatures)

title: ARIA Xenium Analysis
text:
Regional analysis of oligodendrocyte transcriptional responses revealed spatial heterogeneity that parallels the regional immune activation observed in microglia following Aducanumab treatment.
In cortical oligodendrocytes, differential expression was modest and largely not significant after multiple testing correction, but several genes indicative of inflammatory signaling and cellular stress were nonetheless induced. These included interferon-responsive and inflammatory mediators such as Cxcl10, Ifit1, Ifit2, and Acod1, together with early stress-response genes such as Jun and Rhob, suggesting that cortical oligodendrocytes are exposed to inflammatory cues but may not represent the primary site of glial activation. Consistent with this relatively mild cortical response, the regional microglial gene set enrichment analysis showed no significant immune pathway enrichment in the cortex.
In contrast, regions exhibiting strong microglial immune activation—particularly the hippocampus, hypothalamus, and white matter—were enriched for cytokine-responsive programs, including IL-15, IL-18, TNF, and interferon signaling pathways. The leading-edge genes driving these enrichments included Ccl2, Ccl12, Cxcl10, Ifit1, Ifit2, Il1b, and C3, indicating the presence of a cytokine-rich inflammatory microenvironment.
Such signals are well known to influence oligodendrocyte biology and likely contribute to the broader transcriptional responses observed in oligodendrocytes, including alterations in lipid metabolism and membrane remodeling genes such as Abca1, Abcg1, Apoc1, and Apod, together with changes in metabolic enzymes including Got1, Ldhb, and Pfkm.
Taken together, these regional differences support a model in which oligodendrocyte responses are secondary to spatially restricted microglial activation triggered by Aducanumab-mediated plaque perturbation. Regions with strong microglial cytokine programs show the most pronounced inflammatory and metabolic signaling cues that can propagate to oligodendrocytes, whereas cortical oligodendrocytes exhibit only subtle transcriptional changes consistent with lower levels of immune activation.

title: ARIA Xenium Analysis
text:
Regional analysis of oligodendrocyte transcriptional responses revealed spatial heterogeneity that parallels the regional immune activation observed in microglia following Aducanumab treatment.
In cortical oligodendrocytes, differential expression was modest and largely not significant after multiple testing correction, but several genes indicative of inflammatory signaling and cellular stress were nonetheless induced. These included interferon-responsive and inflammatory mediators such as Cxcl10, Ifit1, Ifit2, and Acod1, together with early stress-response genes such as Jun and Rhob, suggesting that cortical oligodendrocytes are exposed to inflammatory cues but may not represent the primary site of glial activation. Consistent with this relatively mild cortical response, the regional microglial gene set enrichment analysis showed no significant immune pathway enrichment in the cortex.
In contrast, regions exhibiting strong microglial immune activation—particularly the hippocampus, hypothalamus, and white matter—were enriched for cytokine-responsive programs, including IL-15, IL-18, TNF, and interferon signaling pathways. The leading-edge genes driving these enrichments included Ccl2, Ccl12, Cxcl10, Ifit1, Ifit2, Il1b, and C3, indicating the presence of a cytokine-rich inflammatory microenvironment.
Such signals are well known to influence oligodendrocyte biology and likely contribute to the broader transcriptional responses observed in oligodendrocytes, including alterations in lipid metabolism and membrane remodeling genes such as Abca1, Abcg1, Apoc1, and Apod, together with changes in metabolic enzymes including Got1, Ldhb, and Pfkm.
Taken together, these regional differences support a model in which oligodendrocyte responses are secondary to spatially restricted microglial activation triggered by Aducanumab-mediated plaque perturbation. Regions with strong microglial cytokine programs show the most pronounced inflammatory and metabolic signaling cues that can propagate to oligodendrocytes, whereas cortical oligodendrocytes exhibit only subtle transcriptional changes consistent with lower levels of immune activation.

title: ARIA Xenium Analysis
text:
If we reconstruct the sequence implied by the DEGs across cell types, the initial trigger most likely occurs in microglia and plaque-associated macrophages, while astrocytes respond secondarily and then propagate the signal.
First, the strongest innate immune activation appears in microglia and BAMs, which upregulate genes such as Nlrp3, Il1b, Axl, C1qa, C1qb, Trem2, Cst7, and Gpnmb. These genes belong to canonical plaque-responsive microglial programs, including inflammasome activation, complement initiation, and lipid sensing. This pattern strongly suggests that the primary sensing of amyloid perturbation caused by Aducanumab occurs in microglia, which is biologically expected because microglia are the main amyloid-clearing cells.
Second, astrocytes show a different transcriptional profile dominated by chemokines and interferon-response genes, including Ccl2, Ccl12, Cxcl10, Ifitm3, Acod1, and Tnf. These genes are typical of astrocytes responding to IL-1β, TNF, or interferon signals, which are precisely the cytokines produced by activated microglia. In other words, the astrocyte program looks like a secondary inflammatory response to myeloid signaling rather than a primary sensing event.
Third, the astrocyte subcluster with the strongest inflammatory program (Cxcl10/Ccl2/Ccl12/Tnf) likely represents the amplification phase of the response. Once induced by microglial cytokines, these astrocytes produce chemokines that further recruit and organize immune cells around plaques. This means astrocytes help expand and spatially organize the inflammatory niche, even though they were not the first cell type activated.
Fourth, oligodendrocytes appear even further downstream. Their DEGs—such as C3, Abca1, Abcg1, Jun, and metabolic changes in Got1, Ldhb, Pfkm—are consistent with tissue remodeling, lipid handling, and metabolic stress, which are typical responses to inflammatory environments rather than drivers of inflammation.
Putting all this together, the most consistent sequence suggested by the data is:

Aducanumab perturbs plaques and activates microglia/BAMs → inflammasome genes, complement initiation, lipid sensing.
Microglial cytokines activate astrocytes → induction of chemokines and interferon-responsive genes.
A chemokine-producing astrocyte subpopulation amplifies the response → recruitment and spatial organization of inflammatory cells.
Oligodendrocytes and vascular cells respond to the inflammatory niche → metabolic, complement, and lipid-remodeling programs.

So the corrected conceptual model is:
microglia initiate → astrocytes amplify → oligodendrocytes and vascular cells remodel
In other words, astrocytes are not the origin of the inflammatory response, but they may still be critical drivers of its propagation and spatial organization once the process has started.
If you want, I can also help turn this into a clear mechanistic model paragraph for the Discussion, which usually reads slightly differently from a Results paragraph and is where reviewers expect this kind of interpretation.

title: Biological interpretation of DEGs across cell types and brain regions
text:
Cd74 — MHC class II invariant chain; upregulated in microglia (Cortex) and endothelial cells (Cortex). This is a hallmark of antigen-presenting cell activation. Its upregulation in both cell types suggests aducanumab triggers a coordinated immune response, consistent with antibody-mediated amyloid clearance engaging the MHC-II pathway.
H2-Eb1 — MHC class II beta chain; up in microglia (Cortex). Co-upregulation with Cd74 reinforces that cortical microglia shift toward an antigen-presenting phenotype under treatment.





Ifitm2, Ifitm3 — Interferon-induced transmembrane proteins; up in astrocytes (Hippocampus). These are canonical interferon-stimulated genes. Their upregulation points to an interferon-driven inflammatory response in hippocampal astrocytes.
Ifit2 — Another interferon-stimulated gene; up in astrocytes (Hippocampus). Together with the Ifitm genes, this defines a clear type-I interferon signature.
Lgals3bp — Galectin-3-binding protein; up in microglia (Hypothalamus). Involved in innate immune signaling and often upregulated in neuroinflammatory states.





Ccl12 — CC chemokine ligand 12 (monocyte chemoattractant); up in microglia (Hippocampus, WM) and astrocytes (Hippocampus). Shared across two cell types in the hippocampus — this is a cross-cell-type conserved response suggesting active immune cell recruitment. Relevant to ARIA, where perivascular inflammation and immune cell infiltration are hallmarks.





C3 — Complement component 3; up in endothelial cells (Hypothalamus). Central to the complement cascade. Endothelial C3 upregulation suggests vascular complement activation, which is directly relevant to ARIA pathophysiology (complement-mediated vascular damage during amyloid clearance).





Abca1 — ATP-binding cassette transporter A1; up in astrocytes (Hypothalamus) and oligodendrocytes (Hypothalamus). Major cholesterol efflux transporter. Shared upregulation across two cell types suggests a coordinated lipid remodeling response.
Abcg1 — Another ABC cholesterol transporter; up in oligodendrocytes (Hypothalamus). Works with Abca1 in reverse cholesterol transport.
Apod — Apolipoprotein D; up in astrocytes (Hypothalamus) and oligodendrocytes (Hypothalamus). Lipid carrier involved in neuroprotection and myelin maintenance. Another cross-cell-type hit.
Srebf1 — Sterol regulatory element-binding factor 1; up in oligodendrocytes (Hypothalamus). Master transcription factor for lipid/cholesterol synthesis. Together with the ABC transporters, this defines a strong lipid metabolism signature in hypothalamic oligos.
Plin4 — Perilipin 4; up in astrocytes (Hypothalamus). Lipid droplet coat protein — suggests lipid droplet accumulation in astrocytes.
Hacd2 — Hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydratase 2; up in astrocytes (Hypothalamus). Involved in fatty acid elongation.





Aspa — Aspartoacylase; up in oligodendrocytes (Hypothalamus). Essential for myelin lipid synthesis (breaks down NAA to acetate for lipid production).
Cldn11 — Claudin-11; up in OPCs (WM). A tight junction protein critical for myelin sheath formation. Suggests OPC maturation or myelination response in white matter.





Mdh1, Mdh2 — Malate dehydrogenases (cytoplasmic and mitochondrial); down in microglia (WM, Hypothalamus) and oligodendrocytes (Hypothalamus). Core TCA/malate-aspartate shuttle enzymes. Downregulation suggests metabolic reprogramming — consistent with immune-activated cells shifting from oxidative phosphorylation toward glycolysis.
Hk1 — Hexokinase 1; down in oligodendrocytes (Hypothalamus). First step of glycolysis. Combined with Mdh2 downregulation, points to broad metabolic suppression.
Got1 — Glutamic-oxaloacetic transaminase 1; down in oligodendrocytes (Hypothalamus). Malate-aspartate shuttle enzyme, same metabolic theme.
Acsl6 — Long-chain acyl-CoA synthetase 6; down in oligodendrocytes (WM). Involved in fatty acid activation for lipid metabolism.





Snap25, Lypd1, Opcml, Grin2a — down in oligodendrocytes; Lamp5, Slc17a7 — down in endothelial cells. These are neuronal genes (synaptic, glutamatergic). Their “downregulation” in non-neuronal cell types likely reflects reduced ambient RNA contamination in treatment samples rather than true biological signal, or SPLIT decontamination artifacts.





Spp1 (Osteopontin) — up in endothelial (Hippocampus). Secreted glycoprotein involved in inflammation and vascular remodeling; associated with disease-associated microglia (DAM) signatures.
App — Amyloid precursor protein; down in microglia (WM). Directly relevant to the amyloid pathway — potentially reflecting feedback from antibody-mediated amyloid clearance.
Cd63 — Tetraspanin; up in oligodendrocytes (Hypothalamus). Exosome/lysosome marker, suggests increased vesicular trafficking.
Gfap — Glial fibrillary acidic protein; up in oligodendrocytes (Hypothalamus). Typically an astrocyte marker — its appearance in oligos may reflect ambient RNA or reactive gliosis signal.
Agt — Angiotensinogen; up in astrocytes (Hippocampus). Astrocyte-secreted, involved in blood-brain barrier regulation.
Hsd11b1 — 11-beta-Hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase 1; down in astrocytes (Hippocampus). Converts inactive cortisone to active cortisol — downregulation may reduce local glucocorticoid signaling.

The overall picture is consistent with aducanumab-induced neuroinflammation: immune activation (MHC-II, interferon, complement, chemokines) coupled with lipid remodeling (likely related to myelin/membrane repair) and metabolic reprogramming. The hypothalamus shows the strongest response in oligodendrocytes, while hippocampus is the hotspot for astrocyte interferon signaling. The cross-cell-type sharing of Ccl12, Abca1, and Apod suggests coordinated tissue-level responses rather than isolated cell-autonomous effects.

title: Microglia Cell proportions
text:
Code
# BiocManager::install("speckle")
# https://bioconductor.org/packages/devel/bioc/vignettes/speckle/inst/doc/speckle.html
library(qs2)
library(Seurat)
library(tidyverse) 
library(speckle)
library(kableExtra)

immune <- UpdateSeuratObject(qs_read("seurat_objects/20260225-mg_kai.qs2"))
Idents(immune) <- "sub.cluster"
# Order samples by Treatment.Group so groups are adjacent
immune$sample_ID <- factor(
  immune$sample_ID,
  levels = c("KK4_465", "KK4_504", "KK4_496", "KK4_492", "KK4_502", "KK4_464"))
Code
p <- plotCellTypeProps(immune,
                  clusters = Idents(immune),
                  sample = immune$sample_ID)

# Enforce sample_ID factor order on x-axis
sample_order <- c("KK4_465", "KK4_504", "KK4_496", "KK4_492", "KK4_502", "KK4_464")
p + scale_x_discrete(limits = sample_order) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.title = element_text(size = 14),
        axis.text.x = element_text(size = 13, angle = 30, hjust = 1),
        axis.text.y = element_text(size = 13),
        legend.text = element_text(size = 14))
The function propeller from the speckl package tests whether cell-type proportions differ between experimental conditions, while properly accounting for biological replication and sample-to-sample variability.
As a result, propeller identifies true compositional changes in cell populations and avoids false positives driven by uneven cell capture or outlier samples.
Transform In propeller, transform refers to converting raw cell-type proportions into a scale where statistical testing is valid. Proportions are bounded between 0 and 1 and have unequal variance, so transforming them stabilizes variance and allows linear models to be used appropriately.
Logit The logit transform maps proportions from [0,1] to \((-\infty, +\infty)\) using
\(\log\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)\)
This reduces heteroskedasticity, improves power to detect differences in cell-type abundance, and makes effects interpretable as differences in log-odds, while small offsets are used to handle zeros. ***
Robust In propeller, robust refers to using robust empirical Bayes variance estimation in the linear model. This down-weights the influence of outlier samples with extreme cell-type proportions, preventing them from artificially inflating significance.
In practice, robust = TRUE makes the test more resistant to technical or biological outliers while preserving group means, leading to more reliable inference on cell-type composition differences.

section: Test for differences in immune cell type proportions between treatment groups in total brain
text:
Test for differences in immune cell type proportions between treatment groups in total brain


Code
# The propeller function can take a SingleCellExperiment object or Seurat object as input 
# and extract the three necessary  pieces of information from the cell information stored in colData.
#  The three essential pieces of information are

# cluster (Idents function by default)
# sample
# group

prop <- propeller(
  x = immune,
  clusters = Idents(immune),
  sample = immune$sample_ID,
  group = immune$Treatment.Group,
  trend = FALSE,
  robust = TRUE,
  transform = "logit")

prop |> as_tibble () |> 
  kbl(digits = 3) |>

Interpretation







Column
Brief explanation




BaselineProp.clusters
Cell type or cluster being tested for proportional differences between conditions.


BaselineProp.Freq
Overall mean proportion of the cluster across all samples, independent of condition.


PropMean.Adu
Mean sample-level proportion of the cluster in the Adu group.


PropMean.IgG
Mean sample-level proportion of the cluster in the IgG group.


PropRatio
Ratio of mean proportions between groups (Adu / IgG); values >1 indicate enrichment in Adu, <1 depletion.


Tstatistic
Moderated t-statistic testing whether transformed proportions differ between conditions; sign indicates direction.


P.Value
Raw p-value for the difference in proportions for that cluster.


FDR
False discovery rate–adjusted p-value accounting for testing across all clusters.

Code
# Wrap propeller so that regions where the model fails return NULL instead of an error
safe_propeller <- possibly(propeller, otherwise = NULL)

# Get all annotated regions, dropping NA entries
regions <- unique(immune$Region) |> na.omit() |> as.character()

# Run propeller separately for each brain region
prop_by_region <- map(regions, \(reg) {
  # Subset to cells from this region only
  sub <- subset(immune, Region == reg)

  # Skip if either treatment group has < 2 samples (propeller requires replication)
  n_per_group <- table(
    distinct(sub@meta.data, sample_ID, Treatment.Group)$Treatment.Group
  )
  if (any(n_per_group < 2)) return(NULL)

  # Test for proportion differences between Adu and IgG within this region
  safe_propeller(
    x         = sub,
    clusters  = Idents(sub),
    sample    = sub$sample_ID,
    group     = sub$Treatment.Group,
    trend     = FALSE,
    robust    = TRUE,       # down-weights outlier samples
    transform = "logit"     # variance-stabilising transform for bounded proportions
  )
}) |>
  # Name list elements by region, then drop NULLs (skipped/failed regions)
  set_names(regions) |>
  compact()

# Combine per-region results into a single data frame with a Region column
prop_region_df <- imap(prop_by_region, \(tbl, reg) {
  as_tibble(tbl) |> mutate(Region = reg)
}) |>
  list_rbind()




Code
prop_heatmap <- prop_region_df |>
  mutate(
    # Convert PropRatio (Adu/IgG) to log2 scale; set Inf/NaN (zero in one group) to NA
    log2ratio = case_when(
      is.nan(log2(PropRatio)) | is.infinite(log2(PropRatio)) ~ NA_real_,
      TRUE ~ log2(PropRatio)
    ),
    # Overlay significance stars on coloured tiles, and × on grey (zero-proportion) tiles
    stars = case_when(
      is.na(log2ratio)   ~ "\u00d7",   # cross for 0-proportion tiles
      P.Value < 0.001    ~ "***",
      P.Value < 0.01     ~ "**",
      P.Value < 0.05     ~ "*",
      TRUE               ~ ""
    )
  )

# Order cell types by mean log2ratio across regions (Adu-enriched at top)
celltype_order <- prop_heatmap |>
  group_by(BaselineProp.clusters) |>
  summarise(mean_r = mean(log2ratio, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  arrange(desc(mean_r)) |>
  pull(BaselineProp.clusters)

prop_heatmap <- prop_heatmap |>
  mutate(
    CellType  = factor(BaselineProp.clusters, levels = rev(celltype_order)),
    Region    = factor(Region),
    # Use a darker grey for × marks so they remain visible on the grey background
    cross_col = ifelse(is.na(log2ratio), "grey30", "grey15")
  )

# Symmetric colour scale limit (rounded up to nearest integer)
clim <- ceiling(max(abs(prop_heatmap$log2ratio), na.rm = TRUE))

ggplot(prop_heatmap, aes(x = Region, y = CellType, fill = log2ratio)) +
  # Tile fill encodes direction and magnitude of the proportion shift
  geom_tile(colour = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  coord_equal() +
  # Significance stars (or ×) overlaid on each tile
  geom_text(aes(label = stars, colour = cross_col), size = 10, vjust = 0.75) +
  # Pass colour strings directly without mapping to a scale
  scale_colour_identity() +
  # Diverging palette: red = Adu-enriched, blue = IgG-enriched, grey = undefined
  scale_fill_distiller(
    palette   = "RdBu",
    direction = -1,
    limits    = c(-clim, clim),
    na.value  = "grey88",
    name      = "log2(Adu/IgG)"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Cell type proportions by brain region",
    subtitle = "Fill: log2(PropRatio Adu/IgG);  * p<0.05  ** p<0.01",
    x = NULL, y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x     = element_text(angle = 40, hjust = 1, size = 18),
    axis.text.y     = element_text(size = 18),
    panel.grid      = element_blank(),
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 18),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(size = 16, colour = "grey40")
  )

iwalk(prop_by_region, \(tbl, reg) {
  cat("\n\n<details><summary>**", reg, "**</summary>\n\n")
  tbl |>
    as_tibble() |>
    arrange(FDR) |>
    kbl(digits = 3, caption = paste("Region:", reg)) |>
    kable_styling("striped", full_width = FALSE) |>
    print()
  cat("\n\n</details>\n\n")
})

title: Differentially expressed genes in oligodendrocytes across brain regions
section: Pseudobulk DE (Adu vs IgG) per region
text:
Pseudobulk DE (Adu vs IgG) per region
To identify transcriptional changes induced by aducanumab in a spatially resolved manner, pseudobulk differential expression analysis was performed independently for each brain region.
Within each region, raw Xenium counts were summed per biological sample using AggregateExpression, yielding one pseudobulk profile per animal (n = 3 Adu, n = 3 IgG). Differential expression between treatment groups was then assessed using DESeq2, as implemented via Seurat’s FindMarkers interface with test.use = "DESeq2" and no minimum expression filter (min.pct = 0), ensuring all detected genes were considered.
For each eligible region, results are displayed as a volcano plot, highlighting genes reaching statistical significance (Benjamini-Hochberg-adjusted p < 0.1), alongside a table of significant differentially expressed genes (DEGs), ranked by adjusted p-value.

Code
regions <- sort(unique(mg$Region))

de_by_region <- map(regions, \(reg) {
  sub_obj <- subset(mg, Region == reg)

  # Replication guard: DESeq2 needs >=2 samples per treatment group
  n_per_group <- table(
    distinct(sub_obj@meta.data, sample_ID, Treatment.Group)$Treatment.Group
  )
  if (any(n_per_group < 2)) {
    cat("\n\n###", reg, "\n\n_Skipped: fewer than 2 samples per treatment group._\n\n")
    return(NULL)
  }

  bulk <- AggregateExpression(sub_obj, group.by = "sample_ID", return.seurat = TRUE)

  # AggregateExpression converts _ to - (e.g. KK4_465 -> KK4-465)
  sample_meta <- distinct(sub_obj@meta.data, sample_ID, Treatment.Group) |>
    mutate(bulk_id = gsub("_", "-", sample_ID))
  bulk$Treatment.Group <- sample_meta$Treatment.Group[
    match(colnames(bulk), sample_meta$bulk_id)
  ]
  Idents(bulk) <- "Treatment.Group"

  res <- anayet(bulk,
                ident1          = "Adu",
                ident2          = "IgG",
                test_method     = "DESeq2",
                min_pct         = 0,
                p_adj_threshold = 0.1,
                plot_title      = paste("Oligodendrocytes —", reg),
                print_plot      = FALSE)

  cat("\n\n###", reg, "\n\n")

  sig_tbl <- if (nrow(res$significant_results) > 0) {
    res$significant_results |>
      select(p_val, avg_log2FC, p_val_adj) |>
      mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) signif(x, 3))) |>
      arrange(p_val_adj) |>
      tableGrob(theme = ttheme_minimal(base_size = 18))
  } else {
    tableGrob(
      data.frame(Result = "No significant DEGs"),
      theme = ttheme_minimal(base_size = 20)
    )
  }

  print(res$volcano_plot + wrap_elements(sig_tbl) + plot_layout(widths = c(2, 1)))
  res

}) |> set_names(regions) |> compact()


Cortex



Hippocampus



Hypothalamus



Thalamus



WM



Code
saveRDS(
  map(de_by_region, \(res) res$all_results),
  "results/20260223-DEG_oligodendrocytes_byRegion.rds"
)




Code
# Build one-row CSV summarising DEGs per region (gene names + counts)
deg_summary <- imap(de_by_region, \(res, reg) {
  sig <- res$significant_results |> rownames_to_column("gene")
  up_genes   <- sig |> filter(avg_log2FC > 0) |> pull(gene)
  down_genes <- sig |> filter(avg_log2FC < 0) |> pull(gene)
  tibble(
    region = reg,
    up     = paste(up_genes, collapse = ", "),
    down   = paste(down_genes, collapse = ", "),
    total  = nrow(sig)
  )
}) |> list_rbind()

# Pivot to wide format: one column per region per direction
deg_wide <- deg_summary |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = region,
              values_from = c(up, down, total),
              names_glue = "{region}_{.value}") |>
  mutate(cell_type = "Oligodendrocytes", .before = 1)

write_csv(deg_wide, "deg_number/DEG_oligodendrocytes.csv")

library(qs2)

Code
# Load full object 
full    <- qs_read("seurat_objects/20260217_Part2_fullobj.qs2")
astro0  <- subset(full, subset = annotatedclusters == "Astrocyte")
# Keep only the assay needed for fresh SCTransform
DefaultAssay(astro0) <- "Xenium"
astro0 <- DietSeurat(astro0, assays = "Xenium", dimreducs = NULL, graphs = NULL)
# Drop Xenium image/FOV segmentation data; otherwise merge triggers huge future globals
astro0@images <- list()

# SCTransform on all astrocytes together (no split/merge, no integration)
astro <- astro0
astro <- SCTransform(
  astro,
  assay = "Xenium",
  new.assay.name = "SCT",
  verbose = FALSE
)

Cluster identity assessment
Based on the top 5 marker genes per cluster, we assessed whether each cluster represents true astrocytes or contaminating cell types from the initial subsetting.









Cluster
Top markers
Identity
Astrocyte?




0
Agt, Calb2, Apoc1, Pgm1, Folh1
Astrocyte — Agt is a canonical astrocyte marker
Yes


1
Fezf2, Hsd11b1, Vim, Plpp3, Scg3
Astrocyte — Vim and Hsd11b1 are astrocyte-enriched
Yes


2
Lamp5, Slc17a7, Egr1, Grin2a, Vip
Neurons — Slc17a7 (Vglut1) is glutamatergic; Lamp5/Vip are interneuron markers
No


3
Cd209a, Mgl2, Kcnj8, Slc47a1, Mrc1
Perivascular macrophages / pericytes — Mrc1 (CD206), Cd209a, Mgl2 are macrophage markers; Kcnj8 is pericyte
No


4
Nlrp3, Cst7, Cd14, Itgax, Trem2
Microglia (DAM) — classic disease-associated microglia signature
No


5
Mog, Mag, Cldn11, Ermn, Aspa
Oligodendrocytes — all canonical oligodendrocyte markers
No


6
Ido1, Nts, Tac1, Otof, Slc17a6
Neurons — Slc17a6 (Vglut2) and Tac1 are neuronal markers
No


7
Cxcl10, Acod1, Ccl2, Ccl12, Tnf
Reactive astrocytes or immune — Cxcl10/Ccl2 can mark reactive astrocytes, but Tnf/Acod1 suggest immune contamination
Uncertain


8
Cldn5, Emcn, Flt1, Cxcl12, Klf4
Endothelial cells — Cldn5, Flt1, Emcn are canonical endothelial markers
No


9

Ependymal / choroid plexus — Ttr, Ccdc153, Tmem212 are ependymal markers
No

OPCs — Pdgfra is the canonical OPC marker
No

title: Astrocyte subpopulations
text:
Differential expression by treatment
Pseudobulk DESeq2 was performed on each true astrocyte cluster (0 and 1) separately, comparing aducanumab (Adu) vs isotype control (IgG) pooled across all brain regions. Raw Xenium counts were aggregated per biological sample using AggregateExpression, yielding one pseudobulk profile per brain (n = 3 Adu, n = 3 IgG per cluster). Significance threshold: adjusted p-value < 0.1.


Code
source("20260217-helper_functions.R")

# Run pseudobulk DESeq2 for each true astrocyte cluster
DefaultAssay(astro) <- "Xenium"
clusters_of_interest <- c(0, 1, 7)

de_by_cluster <- map(clusters_of_interest, \(cl) {
  # Subset to cells in this cluster
  sub_obj <- subset(astro, seurat_clusters == cl)

  # Pseudobulk aggregation per sample
  bulk <- AggregateExpression(sub_obj, group.by = "sample_ID", return.seurat = TRUE)

  # Map treatment metadata (AggregateExpression converts _ to -)
  sample_meta <- distinct(sub_obj@meta.data, sample_ID, Treatment.Group) |>
    mutate(bulk_id = gsub("_", "-", sample_ID))
  bulk$Treatment.Group <- sample_meta$Treatment.Group[
    match(colnames(bulk), sample_meta$bulk_id)
  ]
  Idents(bulk) <- "Treatment.Group"

  # DESeq2 via anayet helper
  # Relaxed threshold (p_adj < 0.1) for astrocyte subclusters
  anayet(bulk,
         ident1          = "Adu",
         ident2          = "IgG",
         test_method     = "DESeq2",
         min_pct         = 0,
         p_adj_threshold = 0.1,
         plot_title      = paste("Astrocyte cluster", cl, "— Adu vs IgG"),
         print_plot      = FALSE)

}) |> set_names(paste0("cluster_", clusters_of_interest))

# saveRDS(de_by_cluster, "results/20260305-DEG_astrocytes_clusters0-1.rds")




Code
de_by_cluster <- readRDS("results/20260305-DEG_astrocytes_clusters0-1.rds")

# Display volcano plot + significant genes table for each cluster
walk(names(de_by_cluster), \(cl) {
  res <- de_by_cluster[[cl]]
  label <- gsub("_", " ", cl) |> str_to_title()
  cat("\n\n###", label, "\n\n")

  sig_tbl <- if (nrow(res$significant_results) > 0) {
    res$significant_results |>
      select(p_val, avg_log2FC, p_val_adj) |>
      mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) signif(x, 3))) |>
      arrange(p_val_adj) |>
      tableGrob(theme = ttheme_minimal(base_size = 18))
  } else {
    tableGrob(
      data.frame(Result = "No significant DEGs"),
      theme = ttheme_minimal(base_size = 20)
    )
  }

  print(res$volcano_plot + wrap_elements(sig_tbl) + plot_layout(widths = c(2, 1)))
})

title: Differentially expressed genes in Glutamatergic Neuron 4 across brain regions
DEG summary across cell types and brain regions
text:
This report consolidates differential expression results across cell types and five brain regions (Cortex, Hippocampus, Hypothalamus, Thalamus, White Matter).
DE testing. For each cell type and brain region, cells were pseudobulk-aggregated per sample using AggregateExpression() on the Xenium raw-counts assay (integer counts, no normalisation). Differential expression was then tested with DESeq2 via Seurat’s FindMarkers(test.use = "DESeq2"), comparing Adu vs IgG. Regions with fewer than two biological replicates per group were excluded. Genes with adjusted p-value < 0.1 were considered significant.


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(pheatmap)
library(patchwork)
library(UpSetR)

# auto-discover all per-cell-type DEG CSVs
csv_files <- list.files("deg_number", pattern = "\\.csv$", full.names = TRUE)
deg_wide  <- map(csv_files, read_csv, show_col_types = FALSE) |> list_rbind()

# extract region names from the _total columns
regions <- str_remove(names(deg_wide)[str_detect(names(deg_wide), "_total$")], "_total")

# pivot total counts to long format
deg_total <- deg_wide |>
  select(cell_type, ends_with("_total")) |>
  pivot_longer(-cell_type, names_to = "region", values_to = "total") |>
  mutate(region = str_remove(region, "_total"))

# pivot up/down counts to long format
deg_long <- deg_wide |>
  select(cell_type, ends_with("_up"), ends_with("_down")) |>
  pivot_longer(-cell_type, names_to = "col", values_to = "genes") |>
  mutate(
    direction = if_else(str_detect(col, "_up$"), "Up", "Down"),
    region    = str_remove(col, "_(up|down)$")
  ) |>
  select(-col)

# count genes per cell_type/region/direction
deg_counts <- deg_long |>
  mutate(count = map_int(genes, \(x) {
    if (is.na(x) || x == "") 0L else length(str_split_1(x, ",\\s*"))
  })) |>
  select(cell_type, region, direction, count)



Total DEGs per cell type and region
Hierarchically clustered heatmap of total DEG counts (padj < 0.1) across all cell types and brain regions. Rows and columns are clustered to highlight similarities. Numbers inside cells indicate the count of significant DEGs.


Code
# build matrix: rows = cell types, cols = regions; sort rows alphabetically
mat <- deg_total |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = region, values_from = total) |>
  column_to_rownames("cell_type") |>
  as.matrix()
mat <- mat[sort(rownames(mat)), ]

num_mat <- matrix(ifelse(is.na(mat), "", sprintf("%.0f", mat)), nrow = nrow(mat), dimnames = dimnames(mat))

pheatmap(mat,
  color           = colorRampPalette(c("white", "#d73027"))(100),
  display_numbers = num_mat,
  fontsize_number = 14,
  number_color    = "black",
  cluster_rows    = F,
  cluster_cols    = TRUE,
  main            = "Total DEGs by cell type and brain region\n",
  fontsize        = 14,
  fontsize_row    = 12,
  fontsize_col    = 14,
  angle_col       = 45,
  cellwidth       = 50,
  cellheight      = 30
)











Up vs Down DEGs
Dodged bar chart showing the number of upregulated and downregulated DEGs for each cell type, faceted by brain region. This allows comparison of directionality across cell types within each region.


Code
deg_counts |>
  ggplot(aes(x = cell_type, y = count, fill = direction)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~ region, nrow = 1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(Up = "#d73027", Down = "#4575b4")) +
  labs(
    title = "DEGs by direction, cell type, and brain region\n",
    x     = NULL,
    y     = "Number of DEGs",
    fill  = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x      = element_text(size = 11, angle = 45, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
    strip.text       = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold")
  )











Up and Down heatmaps
Side-by-side heatmaps of upregulated and downregulated DEG counts, allowing comparison of directionality patterns across cell types and regions.


Code
# build separate matrices for up and down; sort rows alphabetically
mat_up <- deg_counts |>
  filter(direction == "Up") |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = region, values_from = count, id_cols = cell_type) |>
  column_to_rownames("cell_type") |>
  as.matrix()
mat_up <- mat_up[sort(rownames(mat_up)), ]

mat_down <- deg_counts |>
  filter(direction == "Down") |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = region, values_from = count, id_cols = cell_type) |>
  column_to_rownames("cell_type") |>
  as.matrix()
mat_down <- mat_down[sort(rownames(mat_down)), ]

# shared color ceiling
max_val <- max(c(mat_up, mat_down), na.rm = TRUE)

# draw both heatmaps
p_up <- pheatmap(mat_up,
  color           = colorRampPalette(c("white", "#d73027"))(100),
  breaks          = seq(0, max_val, length.out = 101),
  display_numbers = TRUE,
  number_format   = "%.0f",
  fontsize_number = 14,
  number_color    = "black",
  cluster_rows    = FALSE,
  cluster_cols    = FALSE,
  main            = "Upregulated DEGs\n",
  fontsize        = 14,
  fontsize_row    = 12,
  fontsize_col    = 14,
  angle_col       = 45,
  cellwidth       = 50,
  cellheight      = 30,
  silent          = TRUE
)

p_down <- pheatmap(mat_down,
  color           = colorRampPalette(c("white", "#4575b4"))(100),
  breaks          = seq(0, max_val, length.out = 101),
  display_numbers = TRUE,
  number_format   = "%.0f",
  fontsize_number = 14,
  number_color    = "black",
  cluster_rows    = FALSE,
  cluster_cols    = FALSE,
  main            = "Downregulated DEGs\n",
  fontsize        = 14,
  fontsize_row    = 12,
  fontsize_col    = 14,
  angle_col       = 45,
  cellwidth       = 50,
  cellheight      = 30,
  silent          = TRUE
)

# arrange side by side
gridExtra::grid.arrange(p_up$gtable, p_down$gtable, ncol = 2)











Gene overlap across cell types
For each brain region, identify DEGs shared across multiple cell types. Genes appearing in more than one cell type within the same region may indicate conserved responses to treatment.


Code
# parse gene lists into a tidy data frame
gene_df <- deg_long |>
  filter(!is.na(genes) & genes != "") |>
  mutate(gene = str_split(genes, ",\\s*")) |>
  unnest(gene) |>
  select(cell_type, region, direction, gene)

# for each region, count how many cell types share each gene
shared_genes <- gene_df |>
  distinct(cell_type, region, gene) |>
  group_by(region, gene) |>
  summarise(
    n_cell_types = n(),
    cell_types   = paste(cell_type, collapse = ", "),
    .groups      = "drop"
  ) |>
  filter(n_cell_types > 1) |>
  arrange(region, desc(n_cell_types))

if (nrow(shared_genes) > 0) {
  shared_genes |>
    kableExtra::kbl(
      caption = "DEGs shared across cell types within each brain region",
      col.names = c("Region", "Gene", "# Cell types", "Cell types")
    ) |>
    kableExtra::kable_styling("striped", full_width = FALSE)
} else {
  cat("No DEGs shared across multiple cell types in any region.")
}

# UpSet plot: for each region with shared genes, show cell-type overlaps
# pool all genes across regions for global cell-type overlap view
gene_by_ct <- gene_df |>
  distinct(cell_type, gene) |>
  mutate(present = 1) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = cell_type, values_from = present, values_fill = 0) |>
  as.data.frame()

if (nrow(gene_by_ct) > 1 && ncol(gene_by_ct) > 2) {
  upset(gene_by_ct,
    sets      = setdiff(names(gene_by_ct), "gene"),
    order.by  = "freq",
    text.scale = 1.3,
    mainbar.y.label = "Shared DEGs",
    sets.x.label    = "DEGs per cell type"
  )
} else {
  cat("Not enough overlap data for UpSet plot.")
}

DefaultAssay(mg) <- "Xenium"

title: Differentially expressed genes in OPCs across brain regions
section: Pseudobulk DE (Adu vs IgG) per region
text:
Pseudobulk DE (Adu vs IgG) per region
To identify transcriptional changes induced by aducanumab in a spatially resolved manner, pseudobulk differential expression analysis was performed independently for each brain region.
Within each region, raw Xenium counts were summed per biological sample using AggregateExpression, yielding one pseudobulk profile per animal (n = 3 Adu, n = 3 IgG). Differential expression between treatment groups was then assessed using DESeq2, as implemented via Seurat’s FindMarkers interface with test.use = "DESeq2" and no minimum expression filter (min.pct = 0), ensuring all detected genes were considered.
For each eligible region, results are displayed as a volcano plot, highlighting genes reaching statistical significance (Benjamini-Hochberg-adjusted p < 0.1), alongside a table of significant differentially expressed genes (DEGs), ranked by adjusted p-value.

Code
regions <- sort(unique(mg$Region))

de_by_region <- map(regions, \(reg) {
  sub_obj <- subset(mg, Region == reg)

  # Replication guard: DESeq2 needs >=2 samples per treatment group
  n_per_group <- table(
    distinct(sub_obj@meta.data, sample_ID, Treatment.Group)$Treatment.Group
  )
  if (any(n_per_group < 2)) {
    cat("\n\n###", reg, "\n\n_Skipped: fewer than 2 samples per treatment group._\n\n")
    return(NULL)
  }

  bulk <- AggregateExpression(sub_obj, group.by = "sample_ID", return.seurat = TRUE)

  # AggregateExpression converts _ to - (e.g. KK4_465 -> KK4-465)
  sample_meta <- distinct(sub_obj@meta.data, sample_ID, Treatment.Group) |>
    mutate(bulk_id = gsub("_", "-", sample_ID))
  bulk$Treatment.Group <- sample_meta$Treatment.Group[
    match(colnames(bulk), sample_meta$bulk_id)
  ]
  Idents(bulk) <- "Treatment.Group"

  res <- anayet(bulk,
                ident1          = "Adu",
                ident2          = "IgG",
                test_method     = "DESeq2",
                min_pct         = 0,
                p_adj_threshold = 0.1,
                plot_title      = paste("OPCs —", reg),
                print_plot      = FALSE)

  cat("\n\n###", reg, "\n\n")

  sig_tbl <- if (nrow(res$significant_results) > 0) {
    res$significant_results |>
      select(p_val, avg_log2FC, p_val_adj) |>
      mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) signif(x, 3))) |>
      arrange(p_val_adj) |>
      tableGrob(theme = ttheme_minimal(base_size = 18))
  } else {
    tableGrob(
      data.frame(Result = "No significant DEGs"),
      theme = ttheme_minimal(base_size = 20)
    )
  }

  print(res$volcano_plot + wrap_elements(sig_tbl) + plot_layout(widths = c(2, 1)))
  res

}) |> set_names(regions) |> compact()


Cortex



Hippocampus



Hypothalamus



Thalamus



WM



Code
saveRDS(
  map(de_by_region, \(res) res$all_results),
  "results/20260223-DEG_OPCs_byRegion.rds"
)




Code
# Build one-row CSV summarising DEGs per region (gene names + counts)
deg_summary <- imap(de_by_region, \(res, reg) {
  sig <- res$significant_results |> rownames_to_column("gene")
  up_genes   <- sig |> filter(avg_log2FC > 0) |> pull(gene)
  down_genes <- sig |> filter(avg_log2FC < 0) |> pull(gene)
  tibble(
    region = reg,
    up     = paste(up_genes, collapse = ", "),
    down   = paste(down_genes, collapse = ", "),
    total  = nrow(sig)
  )
}) |> list_rbind()

# Pivot to wide format: one column per region per direction
deg_wide <- deg_summary |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = region,
              values_from = c(up, down, total),
              names_glue = "{region}_{.value}") |>
  mutate(cell_type = "OPCs", .before = 1)

write_csv(deg_wide, "deg_number/DEG_OPCs.csv")

Code
DefaultAssay(mg) <- "Xenium"

title: Differentially expressed genes in endothelial cells across brain regions
section: Pseudobulk DE (Adu vs IgG) per region
text:
Pseudobulk DE (Adu vs IgG) per region
To identify transcriptional changes induced by aducanumab in a spatially resolved manner, pseudobulk differential expression analysis was performed independently for each brain region.
Within each region, raw Xenium counts were summed per biological sample using AggregateExpression, yielding one pseudobulk profile per animal (n = 3 Adu, n = 3 IgG). Differential expression between treatment groups was then assessed using DESeq2, as implemented via Seurat’s FindMarkers interface with test.use = "DESeq2" and no minimum expression filter (min.pct = 0), ensuring all detected genes were considered.
For each eligible region, results are displayed as a volcano plot, highlighting genes reaching statistical significance (Benjamini-Hochberg-adjusted p < 0.1), alongside a table of significant differentially expressed genes (DEGs), ranked by adjusted p-value.

Code
regions <- sort(unique(mg$Region))

de_by_region <- map(regions, \(reg) {
  sub_obj <- subset(mg, Region == reg)

  # Replication guard: DESeq2 needs >=2 samples per treatment group
  n_per_group <- table(
    distinct(sub_obj@meta.data, sample_ID, Treatment.Group)$Treatment.Group
  )
  if (any(n_per_group < 2)) {
    cat("\n\n###", reg, "\n\n_Skipped: fewer than 2 samples per treatment group._\n\n")
    return(NULL)
  }

  bulk <- AggregateExpression(sub_obj, group.by = "sample_ID", return.seurat = TRUE)

  # AggregateExpression converts _ to - (e.g. KK4_465 -> KK4-465)
  sample_meta <- distinct(sub_obj@meta.data, sample_ID, Treatment.Group) |>
    mutate(bulk_id = gsub("_", "-", sample_ID))
  bulk$Treatment.Group <- sample_meta$Treatment.Group[
    match(colnames(bulk), sample_meta$bulk_id)
  ]
  Idents(bulk) <- "Treatment.Group"

  res <- anayet(bulk,
                ident1          = "Adu",
                ident2          = "IgG",
                test_method     = "DESeq2",
                min_pct         = 0,
                p_adj_threshold = 0.1,
                plot_title      = paste("Endotheliala —", reg),
                print_plot      = FALSE)

  cat("\n\n###", reg, "\n\n")

  sig_tbl <- if (nrow(res$significant_results) > 0) {
    res$significant_results |>
      select(p_val, avg_log2FC, p_val_adj) |>
      mutate(across(where(is.numeric), \(x) signif(x, 3))) |>
      arrange(p_val_adj) |>
      tableGrob(theme = ttheme_minimal(base_size = 18))
  } else {
    tableGrob(
      data.frame(Result = "No significant DEGs"),
      theme = ttheme_minimal(base_size = 20)
    )
  }

  print(res$volcano_plot + wrap_elements(sig_tbl) + plot_layout(widths = c(2, 1)))
  res

}) |> set_names(regions) |> compact()


Cortex



Hippocampus



Hypothalamus



Thalamus



WM



Code
saveRDS(
  map(de_by_region, \(res) res$all_results),
  "results/20260223-DEG_endothelial_byRegion.rds"
)




Code
# Build one-row CSV summarising DEGs per region (gene names + counts)
deg_summary <- imap(de_by_region, \(res, reg) {
  sig <- res$significant_results |> rownames_to_column("gene")
  up_genes   <- sig |> filter(avg_log2FC > 0) |> pull(gene)
  down_genes <- sig |> filter(avg_log2FC < 0) |> pull(gene)
  tibble(
    region = reg,
    up     = paste(up_genes, collapse = ", "),
    down   = paste(down_genes, collapse = ", "),
    total  = nrow(sig)
  )
}) |> list_rbind()

# Pivot to wide format: one column per region per direction
deg_wide <- deg_summary |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = region,
              values_from = c(up, down, total),
              names_glue = "{region}_{.value}") |>
  mutate(cell_type = "Endothelial", .before = 1)

write_csv(deg_wide, "deg_number/DEG_endothelial.csv")

title: ARIA Xenium Analysis
text: There is a coherent three-layer model of the transcriptional response to Aducanumab. Microglia and border-associated macrophages constitute the initiation layer, upregulating inflammasome genes such as Nlrp3 and Il1b, complement initiators C1qa and C1qb, and lipid-sensing receptors Trem2, Axl, and Gpnmb. These are canonical markers of plaque-reactive microglia, and their induction is biologically expected because microglia are the primary amyloid-clearing cell type. Astrocytes constitute the amplification layer, responding to microglial cytokines with a chemokine and interferon program that includes Ccl2, Ccl12, Cxcl10, Ifitm3, Acod1, and Tnf. A discrete inflammatory astrocyte subcluster drives this amplification, spatially organizing immune cell recruitment around perturbed plaques. Oligodendrocytes represent the remodeling layer, mounting the largest transcriptional response of any cell type in terms of gene count, but through complement induction (C3), lipid-handling programs (Abca1, Abcg1, Apod), and metabolic downregulation (Got1, Ldhb, Pfkm) rather than through primary inflammatory signaling. Vascular cells, including endothelial cells and pericytes, participate through complement upregulation (C3) and fibroblast-like cells expressing Trem2 and Gpnmb, extending the inflammatory niche to the neurovascular unit. The global model is thus: microglia initiate, astrocytes amplify, oligodendrocytes and vascular cells remodel.

title: ARIA Xenium Analysis
text: There is a coherent three-layer model of the transcriptional response to Aducanumab. Microglia and border-associated macrophages constitute the initiation layer, upregulating inflammasome genes such as Nlrp3 and Il1b, complement initiators C1qa and C1qb, and lipid-sensing receptors Trem2, Axl, and Gpnmb. These are canonical markers of plaque-reactive microglia, and their induction is biologically expected because microglia are the primary amyloid-clearing cell type. Astrocytes constitute the amplification layer, responding to microglial cytokines with a chemokine and interferon program that includes Ccl2, Ccl12, Cxcl10, Ifitm3, Acod1, and Tnf. A discrete inflammatory astrocyte subcluster drives this amplification, spatially organizing immune cell recruitment around perturbed plaques. Oligodendrocytes represent the remodeling layer, mounting the largest transcriptional response of any cell type in terms of gene count, but through complement induction (C3), lipid-handling programs (Abca1, Abcg1, Apod), and metabolic downregulation (Got1, Ldhb, Pfkm) rather than through primary inflammatory signaling. Vascular cells, including endothelial cells and pericytes, participate through complement upregulation (C3) and fibroblast-like cells expressing Trem2 and Gpnmb, extending the inflammatory niche to the neurovascular unit. The global model is thus: microglia initiate, astrocytes amplify, oligodendrocytes and vascular cells remodel.

title: ARIA Xenium Analysis
text:
Regional breakdown

Cortex
The cortical response is the most immunologically specific in microglia but silent in astrocytes and oligodendrocytes. Microglia show the only two significant cortical DEGs across all three cell types: Cd74 (FC = 0.55) and H2-Eb1 (FC = 0.53), both upregulated. These are MHC class II antigen-presentation genes, and their co-upregulation defines a phagocytic, antigen-presenting microglial state, consistent with active amyloid clearance at cortical plaque sites. The absence of any significant astrocyte or oligodendrocyte DEGs in the cortex, despite the non-zero nominal signals for Cxcl10, Ifit1, and Jun in oligodendrocytes, indicates that the cortical microglial signal has not propagated to adjacent glia at the statistical threshold tested. The cortex, therefore, represents a region of contained initiation: the primary immune sensor is active, but the amplification and remodeling layers have not engaged.


Hippocampus
The hippocampus is the principal site of astrocyte interferon signaling. Astrocytes there show three significant DEGs: Ifitm3 (FC = 0.48) upregulated, Agt (FC = 0.60) upregulated, and Hsd11b1 (FC = -0.46) downregulated. The induction of Ifitm3, a canonical interferon-stimulated gene, is consistent with the global narrative’s claim that a hippocampal astrocyte subcluster drives interferon and chemokine amplification. The upregulation of Agt, an astrocyte-secreted angiotensin precursor involved in blood-brain barrier regulation, adds a vascular-modulatory dimension specific to this region. The downregulation of Hsd11b1, which converts cortisone to active cortisol, suggests reduced local glucocorticoid signaling, potentially permitting a more sustained inflammatory response by removing one of its natural brakes. In microglia, the hippocampus shows only Ccl12 as significant (FC = 1.20), a monocyte chemoattractant also significantly upregulated in astrocytes of the same region in the global analysis, making it the one cross-cell-type shared signal in the hippocampus. Oligodendrocytes contribute nothing significant here. The hippocampus thus supports the amplification phase of the global model, with astrocytes showing the region’s most coherent biological response.


Hypothalamus
The hypothalamus is the most transcriptionally active region across all three cell types and provides the strongest support for the remodeling layer of the global narrative. Astrocytes show three significant upregulated DEGs: Plin4 (FC = 0.90), Apod (FC = 0.65), and Abca1 (FC = 0.41). These encode a lipid droplet coat protein, a lipid carrier involved in neuroprotection and myelin maintenance, and the major cholesterol efflux transporter, respectively. This is a coherent astrocyte lipid-handling program, not an inflammatory one. Oligodendrocytes in the hypothalamus produce the largest significant regional gene set in the entire dataset: eight upregulated genes, including Abca1 (FC = 0.52), Srebf1 (FC = 0.34), Abcg1 (FC = 0.32), Apod (FC = 0.33), Aspa (FC = 0.24), and Gfap (FC = 0.58), alongside four downregulated metabolic genes, Snap25 (FC = -0.44), Mdh2 (FC = -0.41), Got1 (FC = -0.40), and Hk1 (FC = -0.37). The upregulated set defines a lipid synthesis and cholesterol transport program, with Srebf1 as the master transcriptional regulator and the ABC transporters as its effectors. The simultaneous downregulation of TCA cycle and glycolytic enzymes (Mdh2, Got1, Hk1) indicates metabolic reprogramming away from oxidative phosphorylation, consistent with an energy-state shift in oligodendrocytes under inflammatory stress. The Apod and Abca1 signals are shared between astrocytes and oligodendrocytes in the hypothalamus, making these two genes the clearest evidence of a coordinated cross-cell-type lipid remodeling response. Microglia in the hypothalamus show only one significant DEG, Lgals3bp (FC = 0.58), an innate immune signaling protein associated with neuroinflammatory states. The hypothalamus thus confirms and extends the remodeling layer: oligodendrocytes here undergo the most extensive adaptive transcriptional program, driven by lipid metabolism and metabolic reprogramming rather than by direct inflammatory signaling.


Thalamus
The thalamus is the most transcriptionally silent region in this dataset. No significant DEGs were identified in microglia, astrocytes, or oligodendrocytes after correction for multiple testing. The thalamus therefore neither supports nor contradicts the global model; it simply shows no engagement at this statistical threshold. Whether this reflects true biological quiescence, lower statistical power due to fewer thalamic cells, or a genuinely lower plaque burden in this region in APOE4 mice is not determinable from these data alone.


White matter (WM)
White matter presents an asymmetric pattern in which microglial chemokine signaling is active but oligodendrocyte and astrocyte responses remain largely absent. Microglia show two significant DEGs: Ccl12 upregulated (FC = 1.53, the highest FC in any microglial regional result) and App downregulated (FC = -0.54). The Ccl12 signal in WM microglia is the strongest chemokine signal in the entire regional dataset and was also significant in hippocampal microglia and, in the global analysis, in hippocampal astrocytes, placing it as the most spatially consistent microglial response across regions. The downregulation of App in WM microglia is directly relevant to the mechanism of Aducanumab: it may reflect feedback suppression of amyloid precursor protein expression following antibody-mediated amyloid clearance, or a shift in microglial metabolic state following phagocytic activity. Oligodendrocytes in WM show only one significant gene, Grin2a downregulated (FC = -0.89). This is a glutamatergic receptor subunit, its appearance in oligodendrocytes most likely reflecting reduced ambient neuronal RNA contamination in treatment samples rather than a primary oligodendrocyte response. Astrocytes show no significant DEGs in WM. White matter therefore represents a region of active microglial initiation, specifically chemokine-mediated recruitment signaling, without detectable propagation to astrocytes or oligodendrocytes.

title: ARIA Xenium Analysis
section: Agreement and disagreement with the global narrative
text:
Agreement and disagreement with the global narrative
The regional data are largely consistent with the global three-layer model, but they qualify and sharpen it in important ways.
The initiation layer is confirmed regionally. Microglial DEGs are significant in all regions except the thalamus, and their content, MHC-II in the cortex, Ccl12 in the hippocampus and WM, and Lgals3bp in the hypothalamus, is coherent with plaque-reactive innate immune programs, even if no single region reproduces the full inflammasome-plus-complement signature described in the global analysis. This regional fragmentation of the microglial response is likely a reflection of statistical power in regional subsets rather than genuine absence of the broader program.
The amplification layer is partially confirmed. Hippocampal astrocytes with Ifitm3 align directly with the global narrative’s claim that hippocampal astrocytes carry the interferon signature. However, the expected chemokine amplifiers, Ccl2, Cxcl10, and Tnf, do not reach significance in any regional astrocyte subset, suggesting that the chemokine-producing astrocyte subcluster described in the global analysis is either too sparse to drive regional-level DEG significance or that its signal is diluted by non-inflammatory astrocyte subpopulations in the regional pseudobulk. This is a point of partial disagreement: the global narrative presents the chemokine amplification astrocyte subcluster as a prominent feature, but the regional data show it only through weaker interferon-gene proxies.
The remodeling layer receives its strongest regional support from the hypothalamus, where oligodendrocyte DEGs are numerous, biologically coherent, and shared with astrocytes (Apod, Abca1). The global narrative’s claim that oligodendrocytes are the cell type undergoing the most extensive adaptive remodeling is fully supported in the hypothalamus; it is essentially absent elsewhere, with WM showing only one likely-artifactual oligodendrocyte DEG. This means the remodeling layer described globally is, in practice, largely a hypothalamic phenomenon, at least at the level of statistical detectability in these data.
The most notable regional-specific finding that does not appear in the global summary concerns the lipid remodeling program in hypothalamic astrocytes, which is distinct from the interferon-chemokine program described globally as the defining astrocyte response. Hypothalamic astrocytes show Plin4, Apod, and Abca1, a lipid-storage and cholesterol-efflux program more typically attributed in the global narrative to oligodendrocytes. This suggests that astrocytes in the hypothalamus adopt a metabolic-remodeling role analogous to that of oligodendrocytes in the same region, rather than the inflammatory-amplification role that dominates in the hippocampus. The regional data therefore reveal that astrocyte functional heterogeneity across regions is greater than the global narrative implies, with hippocampal astrocytes acting as immune amplifiers and hypothalamic astrocytes acting as lipid remodelers.

title: Pandoc User’s Guide
text:
Using pandoc
If no input-files are specified, input is read from stdin. Output goes to stdout by default. For output to a file, use the -o/--output option:
pandoc -o output.html input.txt
By default, pandoc produces a document fragment. To produce a standalone document (e.g. a valid HTML file including <head> and <body>), use the -s or --standalone flag:
pandoc -s -o output.html input.txt
For more information on how standalone documents are produced, see Templates below.
If multiple input files are given, pandoc will concatenate them all (with blank lines between them) before parsing. (Use --file-scope to parse files individually.)

title: Pandoc User’s Guide
text:
Specifying formats
The format of the input and output can be specified explicitly using command-line options. The input format can be specified using the -f/--from option, the output format using the -t/--to option. Thus, to convert hello.txt from Markdown to LaTeX, you could type:
pandoc -f markdown -t latex hello.txt
To convert hello.html from HTML to Markdown:
pandoc -f html -t markdown hello.html
Supported input and output formats are listed below under Options (see -f for input formats and -t for output formats). You can also use pandoc --list-input-formats and pandoc --list-output-formats to print lists of supported formats.
If the input or output format is not specified explicitly, pandoc will attempt to guess it from the extensions of the filenames. Thus, for example,
pandoc -o hello.tex hello.txt
will convert hello.txt from Markdown to LaTeX. If no output file is specified (so that output goes to stdout), or if the output file’s extension is unknown, the output format will default to HTML. If no input file is specified (so that input comes from stdin), or if the input files’ extensions are unknown, the input format will be assumed to be Markdown.

title: Pandoc User’s Guide
text:
Character encoding
Pandoc uses the UTF-8 character encoding for both input and output. If your local character encoding is not UTF-8, you should pipe input and output through iconv:
iconv -t utf-8 input.txt | pandoc | iconv -f utf-8
Note that in some output formats (such as HTML, LaTeX, ConTeXt, RTF, OPML, DocBook, and Texinfo), information about the character encoding is included in the document header, which will only be included if you use the -s/--standalone option.

title: Pandoc User’s Guide
text:
Creating a PDF
To produce a PDF, specify an output file with a .pdf extension:
pandoc test.txt -o test.pdf
By default, pandoc will use LaTeX to create the PDF, which requires that a LaTeX engine be installed (see --pdf-engine below). Alternatively, pandoc can use ConTeXt, roff ms, or HTML as an intermediate format. To do this, specify an output file with a .pdf extension, as before, but add the --pdf-engine option or -t context, -t html, or -t ms to the command line. The tool used to generate the PDF from the intermediate format may be specified using --pdf-engine.
You can control the PDF style using variables, depending on the intermediate format used: see variables for LaTeX, variables for ConTeXt, variables for wkhtmltopdf, variables for ms. When HTML is used as an intermediate format, the output can be styled using --css.
To debug the PDF creation, it can be useful to look at the intermediate representation: instead of -o test.pdf, use for example -s -o test.tex to output the generated LaTeX. You can then test it with pdflatex test.tex.
When using LaTeX, the following packages need to be available (they are included with all recent versions of TeX Live): amsfonts, amsmath, lm, unicode-math, iftex, listings (if the --listings option is used), fancyvrb, longtable, booktabs, multirow (if the document contains a table with cells that cross multiple rows), graphicx (if the document contains images), bookmark, xcolor, soul, geometry (with the geometry variable set), setspace (with linestretch), and babel (with lang). If CJKmainfont is set, xeCJK is needed if xelatex is used, else luatexja is needed if lualatex is used. framed is required if code is highlighted in a scheme that use a colored background. The use of xelatex or lualatex as the PDF engine requires fontspec. lualatex uses selnolig and lua-ul. xelatex uses bidi (with the dir variable set). If the mathspec variable is set, xelatex will use mathspec instead of unicode-math. The csquotes package will be used for typography if the csquotes variable or metadata field is set to a true value. The natbib, biblatex, bibtex, and biber packages can optionally be used for citation rendering. If math with \cancel, \bcancel, or \xcancel is used, the cancel package is needed. The following packages will be used to improve output quality if present, but pandoc does not require them to be present: upquote (for straight quotes in verbatim environments), microtype (for better spacing adjustments), parskip (for better inter-paragraph spaces), xurl (for better line breaks in URLs), and footnotehyper or footnote (to allow footnotes in tables).

title: Pandoc User’s Guide
text:
Reading from the Web
Instead of an input file, an absolute URI may be given. In this case pandoc will fetch the content using HTTP:
pandoc -f html -t markdown https://www.fsf.org
It is possible to supply a custom User-Agent string or other header when requesting a document from a URL:
pandoc -f html -t markdown --request-header User-Agent:"Mozilla/5.0" \
  https://www.fsf.org

title: Pandoc User’s Guide
text:
General options

-f FORMAT, -r FORMAT, --from=FORMAT, --read=FORMAT

Specify input format. FORMAT can be:


asciidoc (AsciiDoc markup)
bibtex (BibTeX bibliography)
biblatex (BibLaTeX bibliography)
bits (BITS XML, alias for jats)
commonmark (CommonMark Markdown)
commonmark_x (CommonMark Markdown with extensions)

haddock (Haddock markup)
html (HTML)

the path of a custom Lua reader, see Custom readers and writers below


Extensions can be individually enabled or disabled by appending +EXTENSION or -EXTENSION to the format name. See Extensions below, for a list of extensions and their names. See --list-input-formats and --list-extensions, below.

-t FORMAT, -w FORMAT, --to=FORMAT, --write=FORMAT

Specify output format. FORMAT can be:


ansi (text with ANSI escape codes, for terminal viewing)
asciidoc (modern AsciiDoc as interpreted by AsciiDoctor)
asciidoc_legacy (AsciiDoc as interpreted by asciidoc-py).
asciidoctor (deprecated synonym for asciidoc)
bbcode BBCode
bbcode_fluxbb BBCode (FluxBB)
bbcode_phpbb BBCode (phpBB)
bbcode_steam BBCode (Steam)
bbcode_hubzilla BBCode (Hubzilla)
bbcode_xenforo BBCode (xenForo)
beamer (LaTeX beamer slide show)
bibtex (BibTeX bibliography)
biblatex (BibLaTeX bibliography)
chunkedhtml (zip archive of multiple linked HTML files)
commonmark (CommonMark Markdown)
commonmark_x (CommonMark Markdown with extensions)
context (ConTeXt)
csljson (CSL JSON bibliography)
djot (Djot markup)
docbook or docbook4 (DocBook 4)
docbook5 (DocBook 5)
docx (Word docx)
dokuwiki (DokuWiki markup)
epub or epub3 (EPUB v3 book)
epub2 (EPUB v2)
fb2 (FictionBook2 e-book)
gfm (GitHub-Flavored Markdown), or the deprecated and less accurate markdown_github; use markdown_github only if you need extensions not supported in gfm.
haddock (Haddock markup)
html or html5 (HTML, i.e. HTML5/XHTML polyglot markup)
